observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee, to closely monitor the implementation of planned development projects of the 20% Development Fund and adopt methods to ensure the timely conduct of the procurement process to optimally utilize the fund, and eventually help the Municipality in timely attaining its desired goals and objectives of the said fund.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC coordinated with the LCE and Engineering Office to ensure the implementation of the 20% DF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Municipal
      Accountant to (i) investigate and expedite substantiation of noted difference between the recorded RPT Receivable and SET Receivable; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred Credit accounts duly supported with documents; and (iii) ensure that the recorded Receivable reconcile with the records maintained by the MTO and Assessor’s Office.

Municipal Treasurer to (i) Submit to the Municipal Accountant, at the beginning of the year, a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year based on the updated Taxpayer’s index card; (ii) Update the Taxpayer’s Index Card, and reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Assessor; (iii) prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year; and (iv) post a notice of the delinquency at the main entrance of the provincial municipal hall in a publicly accessible.

We      further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials concerned to implement other tax collection mechanisms and strategies to encourage delinquent taxpayers to settle their tax liabilities and increase RPT collections of the Municipality.

We also recommended the MGSO create and maintain updated Stock Cards for each category of supplies with their respective stock numbers.  These Stock Cards should be reconciled with the RPCI at the end of each year.  In addition, the Municipal Accountant should likewise maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for every stock number and reconcile them with the Stock Cards of the GSO and the RPCI of the Inventory Committee.  Meanwhile, regular reconciliation of records of both offices is necessary.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor centralize the inventory system, stockpile manage by the MGSO, if warranted, or require all department heads to manage the inventories under their custody and prepare the necessary documents to account for regular issuances of these items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

 instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper classification, individual identification, status of serviceability, accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties;

 require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as recommended above; (ii) drop from the PPE account the identified items below the ₱15,000.00 threshold totaling ₱717,242.43; (iv) initiate the proper turn-over of the completed projects to the respective Barangay-Recipients supported with the properly accomplished turn-over documents, and effect adjustments in the books; and (v) maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and

 direct the General Services Officer to take charge in updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and accountability of purchased/ acquired and issued PPEs.

 Lastly, we recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We reiterated the recommendation, with modification, that the Municipal Accountant should: 

 prepare adjusting entry to drop from Accounts Payable account the unsubstantiated claims as well as to effect corrections on the erroneously recorded transactions;

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 

 conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the balances of the Due to NGAs accounts, determine the source agencies and the status of programs, projects and activities for which funds were received and maintain complete records of the same;

 exhaust possible remedy to reconcile the balances of the account and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and

 make representations with identified source agencies for the return of unutilized balances.


We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

 the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the project duration stipulated per contract and exercise diligence in the inspection of completed projects immediately after the reported date of completion to determine whether projects were completed on time, with considerations to Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any and ensure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan; and

 the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidated Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the cost of the unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay.

Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:

 Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and

Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Budget Officer:

 strictly adhere to Section 287 of the Republic Act 7160 and pertinent provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 and relevant/applicable law, rules and regulations to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and for the realization of the intended purpose of the fund; and

 conduct a careful evaluation in preparing Local Development Plan and Annual/Supplemental Investment Plan, respectively, and to itemize the PPAs to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation. Identify, first and foremost, those considered vital and urgent to the Municipality and include in the AIP only those that can be implemented during the year through proper scheduling.

This audit observation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/ Disallowance, if warranted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

 Municipal General Service Officer to determine the LGU properties, with their correct total cost including any improvement cost, not yet covered by insurance and facilitate the filing/application for the necessary insurance coverage of all identified properties with the GSIS under the Property Insurance Fund to ensure that any loss or damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular 2018-02; and 

 Local Legislative Body to include in the appropriation ordinance of the Municipality the amount necessary for the insurance coverage of LGU properties.

We reiterated our audit recommendations in CYs 2017 and 2020 with modification, that the Municipal Accountant:

Prepare adjusting entry to drop from the Accounts Payable unsubstantiated claims totaling P36,497,012.81;

Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods and services charged from the current year appropriation to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the municipality; and

Keep the original file of the vouchers and its supporting documents pertaining to all recorded Accounts Payable until it is paid.

 Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have complete supporting documents.

Stop the practice of paying prior year’s obligations which were not recorded as Accounts Payable.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
 require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract. 

 record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the Municipality and to recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of year-end.

 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer initiate the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation which were reverted to the surplus of the General Fund should there still be payment of prior year’s unpaid obligations not recorded as payables, to avoid the erroneous charging of prior years’ expenditures to the current year’s appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency:

 henceforth, prepare the Monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (MRSMI), duly supported with properly accomplished RIS.

 instruct all driver personnel of the municipality to properly accomplish the Driver’s Trip Tickets and prepare the report of official travel monthly and to submit the same to the General Services Officer for the preparation of the monthly report of fuel consumption. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense account the fuel, oil and gasoline procured by the municipality. Recognize expense based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Treasurer should: 

 maintain a record on estimated and actual collections and the disbursements therefrom, to make sure that such disbursements will not exceed fifty percent (50%) of the uncollected estimated revenue accruing to the General Fund.

 We also reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Accountant should assist the Municipal Treasurer in monitoring the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank balances by tallying their respective records every end of the month to ensure that the LGU does not incur any deficit and overdraft.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor should:
 together with the Department Heads, exercise fiscal restraint and practice austerity measures to conserve the minimal resources of the LGU, including the approval of disbursements to the most basic, urgent needs only.

We reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and other LGU officials submit a written explanation why the appropriation for the 20% DF was not used to implement the programmed 20% DF projects and why was it utilized for other purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal DRRMO should: 

maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF by implementing the PAPs in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to strengthen the capability of the Municipality to cope with adverse effects of disasters and/or pandemic, and 

include in their LDRRMF Investment Plan the PAPs that are to be undertaken using the LDRRMF unexpended balances in the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the MPDC prepares a Supplemental AIP integrated therein the COVID 19 related PPAs, to be approved by the Sanggunian, to legally use the unexpended balances and to avoid disallowance of the transactions in audit.

We reiterated our recommendation in CY 2017 AAR, with modification, that the Municipal Accountant should: 

prepare adjusting entry to drop from Accounts Payable unsubstantiated claims totaling P31,087,632.70 unless valid supporting documents are presented; and 

keep the original file of the vouchers and its supporting documents pertaining to all recorded Accounts Payable until it is paid, and stop the practice of paying prior year’s obligations which were not recorded as Accounts Payable.

We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer stop the practice of releasing DVs to payees or suppliers and ensure that all the DVs which remained unpaid as of yearend, and duly obligated in the RAO, are transmitted to the Municipal Accountant before the agreed cut-off date to allow recording of the same in the books.

We recommended that the LGU should reconcile first with PASAR the latter’s tax obligation, with due consideration of its advance remittances, in order that the correct income be recognized and the other deferred credits be adjusted, before making any request for advances for its 2% share of the gross income of PASAR.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry/ies for the noted variances and for the recognition of the earned portion of the advanced collections whenever appropriate.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer should: 

maintain a record on estimated and actual collections and the disbursements therefrom, to make sure that such disbursements will not exceed fifty percent (50%) of the uncollected estimated revenue accruing to the General Fund.

We also reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Accountant should assist the Municipal Treasurer in monitoring the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank balances by tallying their respective records every end of the month to ensure that the LGU does not incur any deficit and overdraft.  

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor should: 

together with the Department Heads, exercise fiscal restraint and practice austerity measures to conserve the minimal resources of the LGU, including the approval of disbursements to the most basic, urgent needs only.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other LGU officials submit a written justification why the appropriation for 20%DF was not used to implement the programmed 20% DF projects and was utilized for other purposes.

We recommended that the MPDC prepares a Supplemental AIP integrated therein the COVID 19 related PPAs, to be approved by the Sanggunian, to legally use the unexpended balances and to avoid disallowance of the transactions in audit.

We recommended that the LCE requires the consultants for Terminal Operation, Local Economic Enterprise and Bulk Water and Port Operation Facilities submit to the Audit Team their deliverables like the comprehensive business plan and physical report of operations for their assigned economic enterprise or the operational plan as stipulated in the Contract of Service;

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee prepares the financial reports for the economic enterprise of the LGU and conduct the semi-annual financial self-sufficiency review for evaluation by the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan.
We recommended that the Municipal Administrator and Secretary to the Mayor should submit valid justification for approving LGU disbursements which were not authorized through an Executive Order.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should submit justification for the grant of meal allowances in cash to aforementioned Punong Barangays, Watchman and Health Officer. We also recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat should submit   to the Auditor for evaluation, justification on the deficiencies noted in the procurement of supplies such as instant noodles, crackers, 3n1 coffee and distilled water.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to enforce liquidation of cash advances granted in prior years by deducting immediately from the salaries of the accountable officers their unliquidated amounts when documents are available to prove the incurrence of the transactions, otherwise, impose legal sanctions on the officials and employees who refuse to abide with the regulations.

Direct all officials and employees to submit Liquidation Reports on their cash advance when the purpose has been served, and within the period prescribed by law.

update the Taxpayer’s Index Card and reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Assessors;

prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including reasons for such non-collection, and;

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant trace records on the unknown balances or prepare necessary adjustments, if found that these were improperly recorded under Due to NGAs account.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee complete the physical count of all properties of the Municipality every year, prepare the required RPCPPE, reconcile the same with Accounting records and submit the reconciled RPCPPE to COA.

File with COA a request for authority to write-off cash advances granted to already deceased Accountable Officers;
Stop the grant of additional cash advances to officials and employees with previous cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all department heads exercise prudence in the disbursement of LGU funds. Limit disbursements to priority expenditures only based on the approved Annual Budget.

Prioritize the issuance of checks for the remittance of all RPT shares due to Barangays concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, and other LGU officials concerned, accelerate the acquisition of a lot for the municipal sanitary landfill. If not yet available, comply strictly with the minimum requirements in the operation of the LGU’s controlled dumpsite to safeguard the environment and health of its constituents.

Impose liquidated damages on payments to contractors the amount equal to at least one tenth (1/10) of one percent of the cost of the unperformed portion of works for every day of delay pursuant to Item 8.1 of Annex “E” of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We also recommended Municipal Accountant submit immediately the Report of Disbursement and Fund Utilization Reports to the identifiable source agencies on all completed programs/projects including information on the remittance of the unused balances.

We recommended that an explanation and authority be submitted by the Municipal Accountant for the charging of the noted expenditures directly from the Due to LGUs – 25% Barangay Share account of the LGU.
We recommended for the refund of the excessive travelling expenses of P167, 328.00 by the participants of the educational tour to Batangas and Oriental Mindoro.
We recommended that the LCE should require the officer/s concerned to strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-002 and COA Circular 96-010.

The LCE should instruct the municipal assessor to coordinate with and to request from the municipal accountant the list of all land, buildings and land improvements constructed/acquired by the municipality.  Conduct ocular inspection of these properties to validate existence, utilization and condition for issuance of DRPs for correction of information thereat.  Preparation and issuance of DRPs should be prioritized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal                         	Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to exert diligent efforts                            	to take up the necessary adjustments and/or corrections regarding on the 	aforecited audit observations and other misstatements thereof, if any.
We recommended further to properly observe the policies laid down on the 	foregoing COA Circular in order to verify timely such account and to 	communicate immediately to management any misstatements and/or deficiencies   	found thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant,  in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to:
Conduct a thorough review of the subject transaction and immediately initiate the necessary adjustments and correcting entries, if warranted, to ensure the fair and accurate presentation of the Property, Plant, and Equipment and Construction in Progress accounts in the financial statements; and
Stop the practice of prematurely recognizing infrastructure expenditures under Property, Plant, and Equipment accounts when the asset is not yet completed, not available for use, or when the probability of future economic benefits or service potential flowing to the Municipality has not been established. Future transactions should strictly adhere to the recognition criteria set forth under IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2020-002 to ensure compliance with applicable accounting and financial reporting standards.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal                      	Accountant to:
Revisit the foregoing transactions if the recording in the books of accounts of the same was aligned with the aforecited COA Circular.  If warranted, provide the necessary corrections and/or adjustments thereof;
Ensure that tangible items which meet the definition and recognition criteria of PPE but its cost is below Fifty Thousand Pesos (P50,000.00) shall be accounted in the books of accounts of the agency as semi-expendable property; and
Continuously adhere with the policies and guidelines outlined in the  aforementioned COA Circular and other applicable laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, together with the concerned local officials, take the following actions:
Exert earnest and proactive efforts to ensure that the PPAs funded under the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and ready for procurement and implementation;
Ensure that all payments for development projects charged against the 20% DF are supported by corresponding appropriations under the same fund; and
Consistently adhere to the policies and guidelines outlined in the aforementioned JMC, as well as other relevant laws, rules, and regulations.  Sustained compliance will contribute significantly to the realization of the municipality’s socio-economic development goals and environmental objectives, ultimately benefiting its constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to:
Transfer the unexpended balances of DRRM-MOOE and the QRF to its Special Trust Fund in consonance with the aforecited COA Circular; and
Submit the distribution list, if available, for the pre-positioned or relief goods to the Audit Team to facilitate the verification and/or examination thereof.
Finally, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local Officials concerned continuously adhere to the policies set forth in any applicable laws and COA Circulars, insofar as LDRRMF is concerned, to further strengthen the capabilities of the local government unit and its communities in disaster preparedness, mitigation, response, and recovery.

Moving forward, we recommended the continued adherence to the provisions  	of the aforementioned Joint Memorandum Circular to further advance           gender equality and women’s empowerment, and to ensure the full                   realization and protection of their legal rights.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the local officials         	concerned to:
Post the utilization report of SEF Budget in the LGU website and/or in at least three conspicuous public places within its territorial jurisdiction;
Submit both the quarterly and annual SEF Utilization Reports to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, copy furnished the Sangguniang Bayan and the Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG; and
Furnish a copy of the SEF Budget to the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer which serve as a basis for disbursement, certification of fund availability, and proper recording, in accordance with the aforementioned Joint Circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal  	General Services Officer to:
Conduct a comprehensive inventory of all municipal properties that remain uninsured under the GSIS Property Insurance Fund;
Facilitate and expedite the insurance application process for all uninsured properties, including those enumerated in this report; and
Ensure that sufficient funds are appropriated in the annual budget to cover  	the cost of insurance premiums, and remit the necessary amounts to the GSIS in a timely manner, in compliance with Republic Act No. 656.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the local officials        	concerned to:
Facilitate and/expedite the proper implementation of the foregoing financial assistance; and the prior years’ grant from national government agencies, if any; and
Strictly adhere to the applicable rules and regulations relative to implementation, utilization and liquidation of the financial assistance from national government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to take up the necessary adjusting journal entries relative to the above-stated adjustments.  Upon recording of these adjustments, ensure that they are supported with complete supporting documents.

   We recommended further, for future reference, that the Municipal Treasurer send written notices to the concerned payees before the checks become stale.  The Municipal Accountant is likewise advised to take up the necessary adjustment in its books of accounts for stale checks.  Moreover, for checks that become stale and require replacement, a new check may be issued upon submission of the stale check to the Municipal Treasurer.  The replacement check shall also be reported chronologically in the Report of Check Issued prepared at the period of cancellation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary adjustment in its books of accounts relative to the foregoing transactions affecting PPE and other related accounts.

We recommended further to perform the following: 

   stop the practice of recording the 15% advance payment to contractors as PPE;

   evaluate the nature of transactions involving PPE in order to record them under their appropriate PPE account; and 

   stop also the practice of recording ordinary repairs and maintenance expenses as PPE.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the understatement of the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer and the overstatement of the Due from Officers and Employees accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the overstatement of the Due to NGAs account and the understatement of the Due to LGU account.

We recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of charging to the 20% LDF expenditures that do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures, and strictly observe the foregoing JMC regarding the use of the said fund.

We recommended that the Municipality adhere to the provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 pertaining to warranty security, not only in infrastructure projects but also in the procurement of goods, in order to protect not only the interest of the government but also those of the third parties involved in the execution of the government projects.

 We recommended further that future procurement of goods be deducted with at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) either in the form of retention money or a special bank guarantee in accordance with the foregoing provision of law.

We recommended that the Municipality ensure that the complete detailed specifications of the items procured are indicated in the Purchase Order, in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010 to protect the interest of the government.

 We recommended further for management to strictly observe the foregoing policy on the submission of PO which is within five (5) working days from issuance thereof, containing all the necessary information of the items procured.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the MSWDO to submit the noted lacking supporting documents and strictly observe the required documentation prescribed under the foregoing laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended further, for future reference, to examine meticulously the rules and regulations on the grant, utilization, liquidation, and reporting of financial assistance received from the National Government Agencies.

In addition, we also recommended the submission of the required monthly reports on fund utilization together with the relevant supporting documents in compliance with the existing law, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
 Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have complete documents to support valid claim.

 Review and reconcile monthly the LDRRM Fund Utilization Reports with the accounting records to ensure proper monitoring of the available and utilized LDRRM Funds and reflect reliable information in the said report.

 Ensure that amounts in the Utilization Report are correctly summarized and reflected in the Notes to Financial Statements to provide a reliable disclosure of the LDRRM Fund at the end of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, and other LGU officials concerned, accelerate the acquisition of a lot for the municipal sanitary landfill. If not yet available, comply strictly with the minimum requirements in the operation of the LGU’s controlled dumpsite to safeguard the environment and health of its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper classification, individual identification, status of serviceability, accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties;

require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as recommended above; (ii) drop from the PPE account the identified items below the ₱15,000.00 threshold totaling ₱717,242.43; (iv) initiate the proper turn-over of the completed projects to the respective Barangay-Recipients supported with the properly accomplished turn-over documents, and effect adjustments in the books; and (v) maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and

direct the General Services Officer to take charge in updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and accountability of purchased/ acquired and issued PPEs.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 

conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the balances of the Due to NGAs accounts, determine the source agencies and the status of programs, projects and activities for which funds were received and maintain complete records of the same;

exhaust possible remedy to reconcile the balances of the account and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and

make representations with identified source agencies for the return of unutilized balances.

Henceforth, the Municipal Mayor mandate the LGU officials designated as Project-in-Charge to: 

implement immediately the projects in accordance with the timeframe provided for in the MOA to maximize the beneficial use of government projects/program and the eventual return of unused funds, if any; 

prepare the corresponding reports for each of the completed projects and submit the same to the Accountant within one month from completion; and

report to the Municipal Mayor any project/program which cannot be immediately implemented for proper disposition or referral to the Source Agency/ies.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the project duration stipulated per contract and exercise diligence in the inspection of completed projects immediately after the reported date of completion to determine whether projects were completed on time, with considerations to Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any and ensure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan; and

the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidated Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the cost of the unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay.

Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:

Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and

Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Disbursing Officer, as well as the other Accountable Officers timely monitor the effectivity and expiration dates of their respective fidelity bond and see to it that renewal thereof be made before their expiration.  Henceforth, no Accountable Officer shall be allowed to perform their duties and responsibilities with expired fidelity bonds. 

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor require the:

Disbursing Officer to (i) timely liquidate her cash advances in accordance with the pertinent rules and regulations, and (ii) reflect accurate data in the cashbook including the date of liquidation thereof.

Municipal Treasurer shall ensure that no additional cash advances be granted to the DO with outstanding balances thereof in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 to avoid accumulation of unliquidated cash advances, and validate that cash advances granted to DO does not exceed the maximum accountability covered by the fidelity bond in order to protect the interest of the Municipality. 

Municipal Accountant to accurately record the transactions as they occur and prepare the necessary and duly supported adjusting entries to ensure appropriateness and reliability of financial information of each account. 

Finally, we recommend strict compliance with the pertinent rules and regulations in granting and utilization of cash advances provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 and the bonding requirement in Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Budget Officer:
render satisfactory explanation on abovementioned deficiencies and errors/omissions in the utilization and implementation of the 20% Development Fund; 

strictly adhere to Section 287 of the Republic Act 7160 and pertinent provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 and relevant/applicable law, rules and regulations to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and for the realization of the intended purpose of the fund; and

conduct a careful evaluation in preparing Local Development Plan and Annual/Supplemental Investment Plan, respectively, and to itemize the PPAs to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation. Identify, first and foremost, those considered vital and urgent to the Municipality and include in the AIP only those that can be implemented during the year through proper scheduling.

This audit observation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/ Disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

issue an Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations for DRRM or his authorized representative for the receipt of in-kind donations.

designate the office to receive all in-kind donations who will:

establish an inventory management system on receipt, distribution and/or utilization of in-kind donations,
 
prepare the Summary/ List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances every month or every quarter supported by Acknowledgment Receipts, Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users, and Inventory of Remaining Balances, and

submit to the Property Custodian copy of the Acknowledgment Receipt for donated equipment and other tangible items which costs P15,000 or less and has a life of more than a year.

require the designated Property Custodian or duly appointed GSO to:

maintain Stock Cards/ Property Card for all donations in-kind received and update regularly;

maintain Registry of Donated Relief Goods for each kind of inventory items; and

prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for issuance of donated PPEs and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for the issuances of tangible items which costs ₱15,000.00 or less and have a life of more than a year and are not considered as PPE to establish accountability over them and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the PAR and ICS. 

require the departments (MDRRMO, DSWDO and RHU) responsible for the distribution of relief goods and other supplies to submit Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users to the Office referred to in item b.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data from the documents obtained, compare records with Registry of Donated Relief Goods maintained by the Property Custodian or duly appointed GSO for check and balance, and accordingly disclose all necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource and Management Officer to: 

review, assess and evaluate the existing manpower, in coordination with the department heads, to prepare an updated Organizational Structure/Staffing Pattern Plan so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be established, and submit a report with recommendations thereon to the Local Chief Executive; 
limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order contracts to those extremely necessary and deploy only to offices that are actually in need of their services; and

institute contracts per Job Order and incorporate the number of man hours, quantity and duration of the work to be performed by the JO personnel in their respective contracts, and require them to submit quantified accomplishment reports to support payments of wages.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

ensure that all vehicles owned by the Municipality are promptly registered as government vehicles with the LTO under the name of the Municipality, include in the annual budget appropriation for the renewal of registration payment of all vehicles, and registration be made within the period prescribed by the LTO to avoid payment of penalties and other sanctions; and

instruct the Property Custodian or Driver of the Motor Vehicle to stop the operation/use of the vehicles with expired LTO registration.

We also recommended that the duly appointed GSO:

investigate, locate records and submit copies of registration documents for the 15 motor vehicles;

keep the records of registration for all LGU vehicles, monitor their expiry dates and schedule the annual renewal of LTO registration in coordination with the Property Custodian or Driver of the vehicle; and

evaluate any unserviceable vehicles for possible disposal, and in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the disposal procedures unserviceable vehicles, pursuant to pertinent provisions in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee and/or General Service Officer submit within the reglementary period, copies of perfected contracts and Purchase Orders (PO) and all the documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation, to the Audit Team, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We also recommended that the Inspection and Acceptance Committee religiously furnish Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to the Audit Team within 24 hours after the acceptance of the delivered items, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Municipal Accountant to review the completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before forwarding these to the Local Chief Executive for approval.

MGSO/BAC Chairman to ensure submission of complete and appropriate documentation for Shopping and Small Value Procurement required in R.A. 9184 with completely filled-out PR, PO and AIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as the responsible officer, ensure that all paid disbursement vouchers and the supporting documents are properly stamped “PAID”, serially numbered and dates are indicated thereof.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the supporting documents attached to the disbursement voucher, and that the latter is properly accomplished before forwarding the same to the LCE for approval. 

Finally, we recommend that the LCE ensure strict compliance by the officials concerned with the rules and regulations under COA Circular Nos. 92-389 and 78-79, and Volume II of NGAS Manual.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our audit recommendations in CYs 2017 and 2020 with modification, that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare adjusting entry to drop from the Accounts Payable unsubstantiated claims totaling P36,497,012.81;
Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods and services charged from the current year appropriation to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the municipality; and
Keep the original file of the vouchers and its supporting documents pertaining to all recorded Accounts Payable until it is paid.
Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have complete supporting documents.
Stop the practice of paying prior year’s obligations which were not recorded as Accounts Payable.
The Municipal Accountant is amenable to the audit recommendations and will comply with them.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have complete documents to support valid claim.
Review and reconcile monthly the LDRRM Fund Utilization Reports with the accounting records to ensure proper monitoring of the available and utilized LDRRM Funds and reflect reliable information in the said report.
Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office to confirm the proper charging of disbursements from the LDRRMF based on the RAO maintained by that office.
Ensure that amounts in the Utilization Report are correctly summarized and reflected in the Notes to Financial Statements to provide a reliable disclosure of the LDRRM Fund at the end of the year.
The management was amenable to the audit recommendations.

We recommended to make the necessary adjusting entries for the recording of the Fund transfer from DBM to the Trust Fund account and deposit the fund to the Trust fund bank account intended for the purpose.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Disbursement Voucher for the return of the unutilized amount totaling P22,270.00 for the completed projects to the national government through the Bureau of Treasury and submit the required Liquidation Reports to the funding agencies concerned.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer admitted that they were able to use the money to pay for their 13th month pay for CY 2021.  This was then deposited back to the Trust Fund Account in January 2022.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
the Municipal Accountant to submit explanation to the Audit Team for paying CYs 2019 and 2020 expenditures from CY 2021 appropriations;
require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract.
record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the Municipality and to recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of year-end.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer initiate the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation which were reverted to the surplus of the General Fund should there still be payment of prior year’s unpaid obligations not recorded as payables, to avoid the erroneous charging of prior years’ expenditures to the current year’s appropriation.
The management were amenable to our audit recommendations and will comply with what is recommended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency:
require the General Services Officer to submit explanation on the unaccounted fuel (diesel, gasoline and unleaded gasoline).  This is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance, if warranted.
henceforth, prepare the Monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (MRSMI), duly supported with properly accomplished RIS.
instruct all driver personnel of the municipality to properly accomplish the Driver’s Trip Tickets and prepare the report of official travel monthly and to submit the same to the General Services Officer for the preparation of the monthly report of fuel consumption.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense account the fuel, oil and gasoline procured by the municipality. Recognize expense based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.
The Municipal Accountant was amenable to stop the practice of recording immediately to expense the fuel, oil and lubricants and will require for the approved RIS to be submitted for these to be recognized as expensed.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Treasurer should:
prepare a Daily Cash Position Report, segregated by mandatory fund (20% DF, LDRRMF, etc.), and submit the same to the Municipal Mayor to inform him on the availability of cash to support checks that he will sign;
maintain a record on estimated and actual collections and the disbursements therefrom, to make sure that such disbursements will not exceed fifty percent (50%) of the uncollected estimated revenue accruing to the General Fund.
We reiterated the recommendation that the Local Finance Committee should convene at least once a month to discuss and monitor whether the targeted income is attained with actual receipts, and to adjust cash disbursement program for the remaining months, when necessary, to prevent the incurrence of cash overdraft.
We also reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Accountant should assist the Municipal Treasurer in monitoring the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank balances by tallying their respective records every end of the month to ensure that the LGU does not incur any deficit and overdraft.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor should:
through the Local Finance Committee, propose a more realistic budget, taking into consideration, among others, the shortfall of collections in the prior years, full provision for all statutory and contractual obligations of the LGU, without sacrificing mandatory appropriations for developmental projects and disaster risk reduction;
intensify the LGU’s revenue collection efforts, including the collection of prior year’s uncollected revenues; and
together with the Department Heads, exercise fiscal restraint and practice austerity measures to conserve the minimal resources of the LGU, including the approval of disbursements to the most basic, urgent needs only.
During the exit conference conducted, the Budget Officer explained that they were still hoping that the PEZA enterprises could recover for CY 2021 and so they were just estimating a P64 million share from these enterprises to be included in their CY 2021 budget.
The Audit Team explained that the municipality has to follow the guidelines on the budget process which is based on the actual income received by the municipality for the preceding year.  If the municipality could have an additional income for the current year, they could easily make a supplemental budget for the additional income received.
The officers and department heads were amenable to our audit recommendations and will try to implement them.

We reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and other LGU officials submit a written explanation why the appropriation for the 20% DF was not used to implement the programmed 20% DF projects and why was it utilized for other purpose.
The management was amenable to our audit recommendations and hope that their financial situation will improve next year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the MDRRM Council, through the MDRRM Officer:
Ensure that the PPAs in the MDRRMFIP are specific and aligned with the NDRRM Plan and the corresponding performance indicators are definite, measurable, and achievable; and
Monitor and evaluate the Programs/Projects/Activities incorporated in the MDRRM Plan to avoid duplication of programs and identify projects that can address the current state of calamity or any calamity that may possibly arise.  Revise the Plan, if necessary and indicate specific timeframe of implementation.
Include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan the PPAs that are to be undertaken using the LDRRMF unexpended balances in the Trust Fund.
During the exit conference the management were amenable to the audit recommendations and will abide by it.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary adjusting entries to close Due to NGAs and close it to Trust Liability, for the remaining P262,870.10 unutilized BGCM.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant should strictly adhere to the provision of IPSAS I on the fair presentation of the financial statements, which requires the faithful representation of the effects of transactions, other events and conditions, and present it in a manner that provides relevant, reliable, comparable and understandable information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare an adjusting entry to record the cost of the medicines and medical supplies which remained undistributed as at December 31, 2020 to the Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies account in the books of the LGU and to correct the overstatement of expenses in CY 2020 financial statement equivalent to the amount of unused inventory items of P1,082,208.00.

Accordingly, we recommended that the IEH/Coordinate in the preparation of monthly RSMI, to account for the medical, dental and laboratories supplies actually used,  to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording.   

For similar transactions in the future, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant should record the medicines and medical supplies as Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies upon purchase, and recognize the cost of the medicines and medical supplies as expense on the basis of the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued submitted to her by the GSO within five days after the end of the month.

We further recommended that the GSO should conduct a physical count of inventories at yearend, furnish a copy of the RPCI to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for reconciliation of Inventory accounts and submit the required Report to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the General Services Officer should: (a) prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances supported with the Acknowledgment Receipts of the donations-in kind,        
(b) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries, not necessarily in the required form, and 
(c) the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any, and 
(d) prepare the same for all donations received in the future, and 
(e) likewise maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the book value of the donated vehicles and record the same in the books, less depreciation computed from the time of donation.
We further recommended that Department Heads should notify immediately the GSO of all donations directly delivered to their office from other national or private agencies for recording in the books or registries.
obtain from the bank all debit and credit memos to validate deductions/additions made by the banks on the agency bank accounts as basis in recording the transactions.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile monthly the actual deductions in the payroll against the GSIS Billing and the amount remitted to avoid discrepancies therein.
Also, the negative amount of the Due to Pag-IBIG Fund coming from prior years balances should be corrected to bring the liabilities account in the financial statements in order.
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer in coordination with other LGU officials concerned ensure the immediate completion of all on-going projects which already have incurred delays;
Include anticipated unworkable days in estimating the number of days to complete a project to come up with a realistic project duration;

Require personnel of the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct regular monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that infrastructure projects are accomplished in accordance with the terms of the contract and to document any work extension orders/suspension orders, and,

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and other LGU personnel concerned to fast-track the completion of the projects and programs in accordance with the timeline provided for in the MOA.

Also, submit the required reports to the funding agencies concerned, specifically the Liquidation Reports, and return any excess funds to the Bureau of Treasury after completion of the project, if required in the MOA.

    the correct carrying value of the Infrastructure Assets account.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to  reclassify  the
    long term portion of Loans  Payable  -  Current  Liabilities  to  Loans
    Payable – Non-Current Liabilities so  as  to  present  the  liabilities
    fairly.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to : (1)  reconcile
    the balances of the subsidiary ledgers and the schedule  in  the  post-
    closing trial balance with the amount  reported  in  the  Statement  of
    Financial Position to establish the correct account balance of  Due  to
    NGAs as of December 31,  2018,  and;  (2)  avoid  preparing  accounting
    entries without sufficient basis.

We recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  prepare  the  necessary
    accounting entries to rectify the errors affecting  the  Cash  in  Bank
    account; reverse the disbursements covered with stale checks; and avoid
    the repetitive inclusion of the errors and  the  stale  checks  in  the
    subsequent BRS.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send written  notices
    to the payees of the checks  which  have  become  stale  and  determine
    whether these checks are eligible for replacement; and pay the  service
    charges from his personal money the amount deducted  by  the  bank  for
    checks returned by the bank due to insufficient funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

      1.) Send final Demand Letters to  all  Accountable  Officers  for  the
    immediate submission of liquidation reports  duly  supported  with  the
    required documents;

      2.) File with COA a request for authority to write-off  cash  advances
    granted to already deceased Accountable Officers;

      3.) Stop the grant  of  additional  cash  advances  to  officials  and
    employees with previous cash advances.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer: (1) obtain  cash  advances
    equal  to  the  net  amount  of  monthly  payrolls;  (2)   return   any
    excess/unused amount to the account  of  the  LGU;  and  (3)  use  cash
    advances only for its specific purposes.

We also recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  process  the  cash
    advances of the DO only when supported with a payroll, the  net  amount
    of which is equal to the amount of cash advance being granted.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor  and  all  department  heads
    exercise prudence in the disbursement of LGU funds. Limit disbursements
    to priority expenditures only based on the approved Annual Budget.

We also recommended that the Municipal  Treasurer:
    1) prepare a Daily Cash Position Report to inform the  Municipal  Mayor
       on the availability of cash to support checks that he will sign;
    2) issue checks only when there  is  sufficient  cash  to  back-up  the
       payments being made; and
    3) prioritize the issuance of checks for  the  remittance  of  all  RPT
       shares due to Barangays concerned.

We  also  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant   assist   in
    monitoring the Cash in  Treasury  and  Cash  in  Bank  to  ensure  that
    obligations of the LGU for RPT Barangay Share could  be  done.    Also,
    transfer immediately to the Trust Fund succeeding  RPT  shares  of  the
    barangay and remit to the barangay on a quarterly basis within five (5)
    days after the end of each quarter.

We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  Municipal
    Budget Officer,  Municipal  Accountant,  Municipal  Treasurer  and  the
    MDRRMO  to  submit  justification  why  the  cash  pertaining  to   the
    unexpended LDRRMF in CY 2017 and prior years were  utilized  for  other
    purposes.

We reiterated our recommendation that  the  Municipal  Treasurer  monitor
    the regular monthly income received by the LGU and  earmark  or  set
    aside 5% of such collections for LDRRMF  to  prevent  the risk  of
    appropriating the amount for other purposes.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:  (1)  prepare  an
    analysis on the actual income of  the  LGU  for the  abovementioned
    years to determine whether  the  LDRRMF  appropriations  were  actually
    supported  with  sufficient  cash,  and;  (2)  record  the  amount   of
    P20,255,026.11 under Trust Liabilities- DRRM account in the Trust  Fund
    unless sufficient basis has been obtained to prove otherwise.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal  Budget  Officer
    obtain the required authority through an SB Ordinace when making budget
    augmentations.

We recommended  and  the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  reconcile
    monthly the actual deductions in the payroll against the  GSIS  Billing
    and the amount remitted to avoid discrepancies therein.

We recommended  and  the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  reconcile
    monthly the  actual  deductions  in  the  payroll  against  the  amount
    remitted to avoid discrepancies therein.

Also, the negative amount of the Due to  Pag-IBIG  Fund  coming   from
    prior years balances should  be  corrected  to  bring  the  liabilities
    account in the financial statements in order.

We recommended that:

   a) The BAC Chairman require the contractors  of  on-going  infrastructure
      projects to post  additional   Performance  Security  to  include  the
      coverage period of one year from completion date;


   b) The  BAC  Secretariat  examine  the  validity  period  of  Performance
      Security posted by winning bidders upon signing of the  contract,  and
      ensure that the coverage  period  will  include  one  year  after  the
      completion of the project, and;


   c) The Municipal Mayor issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance one year
      after the completion of each project.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor,  and  other  LGU  officials
    concerned, accelerate the  acquisition  of  a  lot  for  the  municipal
    sanitary landfill. If not  yet  available,  comply  strictly  with  the
    minimum requirements in the operation of the LGU’s controlled  dumpsite
    to safeguard the environment and health of its constituents.

We recommended that henceforth the Annual GAD Plan and Budget  of  the
    Municipality  be  submitted  to  the   DILG   for   certification   and
    endorsement.

We also recommended  that  the  Budget  Officer  appropriate  for  GAD
    programs an amount  equivalent to 5% of the total estimated income  for
    the year ; and the GAD Focal Person identify,  programs,  projects  and
    activities for inclusion in the GAD Plan and Budget for the year 2018.

We recommended  that,  the  GAD  Focal  Point  System  headed  by  the
    Municipal Mayor submit the GAD Accomplishment Report of the LGU for  CY
    2018 to the Provincial GAD Focal Point and to DILG RO VIII.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall see to  it  that
    disbursements for GAD related  programs,  projects  and  activities  be
    equivalent to  at  least  5%  of  the  total  estimated  income  of  the
    Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the appropriate
accounting entries to record all the disbursements paid by the bank based on the
Bank Reconciliation Statements previously prepared. Also, record all checks issued
based on the report of the Municipal Treasurer.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit weekly all paid
disbursement vouchers with the supporting documents to the Accounting Office for
recording in the books.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor impose the pertinent
sanctions prescribed by law on non-rendition of accounts if the continued delay in
submission of reports by the Municipal Treasurer persist.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer
to:
1) prepare a Daily Cash Position Report to inform the Municipal Mayor on
the availability of cash to support checks that he will sign;
2) issue checks only when there is sufficient cash to back-up the payments
being made;
3) cancel all stale checks and replace those with valid claims and only when
there is sufficient cash for its payment.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant assist in monitoring
the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank to ensure that the LGU does not incur any
overdraft.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all department
heads exercise prudence in the disbursement of LGU funds. Limit disbursements to
priority expenditures only based on the approved Annual Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit his collections to the
LGU depository bank intact and daily or not later than the next banking day in
adherence to existing laws, rules and regulations to avoid possible occurrence of loss
of LGU funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a.) identify which of the recorded payables have valid claimants by obtaining the
vouchers and its supporting documents;
b.) prepare an entry in the books to close the dormant account payables and
those without supporting documents;
c.) retain the original file of the vouchers and its supporting documents
pertaining to all recorded Accounts Payable until it is paid.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish monthly the
Accountant with a summary of water billings prepared and served to
concessionaires. We also recommend that the Municipal Treasurer intensify its
collection of all billings made especially prior years’ billings to PhilPhos to defray
recurring operating expenses.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant record a
receivable and recognized as income the services rendered by Bulk Water Supply
Facility based on the monthly Summary of Water Bills submitted by the Municipal
Treasurer. Also, record all prior years payables to JE and LEYECO V so that the
financial statements of the LGU will be fairly presented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a.) require the LGU Legal Officer concerned to conduct a comprehensive
review on the operations of the Bulk Water Supply Facility and render a
report thereon;
b.) use the said report to consider its financial impact on the overall LGU
operations and come up with a more prudent decision whether to shut
34
c.) institute legal actions to require the concessionaires immediate payments
of their long outstanding unpaid water bills.
We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of
the LGU all billed water services of the Bulk Water Supply Facility as revenues and
record all expenses, such as the loan amortization, which are related to its
operations in order to come up with a more reliable report on its financial
performance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize the liability of
the Municipality of Isabel to the different National Government Agencies by
recording all fund transfers as Due To NGAs and to record income only when the
intended project or program is completed. Also, we recommend that the Municipal
Accountant prepare correcting entries to reclassify the unutilized funds from NGAs.

We recommended that the Local Development Council, through the Local
Chief Executive incorporate all priority development projects in the current year’s
Annual Investment Program. Any revision on the AIP should be consulted and
approved by the Local Development Council.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Engineer to fast track the completion of all unimplemented projects funded from
the 20% Development Fund and ensure that no project shall be implemented if it is
not in the Annual Investment Program of the current year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Disbursement
Voucher and the Municipal Treasurer prepare the check, in favor of the Bureau of
Treasury, to remit the unutilized balances of previous years’ fund transfers.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit immediately
the Report of Disbursement and Fund Utilization Reports to the identifiable source
agencies on all completed programs/projects including information on the
remittance of the unused balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget
Officer, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and the MDRRMO to submit
justification why the cash pertaining to the unexpended LDRRMF in CY 2016 were
utilized for other purposes.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer monitor the regular
monthly income received by the LGU and earmark or set aside 5% of such
collections for LDRRMF to ensure that the LGU comply with the provisions of the
law.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an analysis on
the actual income of the LGU for the past 5 years to determine whether the
LDRRMF appropriations were actually supported with sufficient cash. The amount
of P9,646,633.69 must be restored in the books of the LGU under Trust Liabilities-
DRRM account unless sufficient basis has been obtained to prove otherwise. Also,
to prepare a JEV to correct the entry in the books on the transfer of unspent
DRRMF to the Trust Fund.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Budget Officer,
Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer submit justification why the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund totaling P1,405,615.00 was utilized
for the project Installation of CCTV Towers which is not a disaster-related project
and not in accordance with the intended purpose of the fund.
We also recommend that the Municipal Budget Officer and MDRRM
Officer ensure that the LDRRMF is spent solely for disaster-related activities as
mandated in the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 20131 and as reflected in the
LGU’s LDRRMF Investment Plan.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, MDRRM Officer and
the Municipal Accountant ensure that the LDRRMF are appropriated and spent
solely for disaster-related activities as contemplated in the NDRRMC, DBM and
DILG’s JMC No. 20131. Allocate funds for honoraria of volunteers and rescuers
from the budget of the office implementing the program unless they render services
during calamities and disasters, or if they undertake pre- and post-disaster
activities.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer ensure the
existence of valid appropriations and adhere to the LDRRMFIP at all times.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Chairman of the Bids
and Awards Committee (BAC) submit to COA the documents pertaining to the
procurement and hiring of the consultants of the municipality. Also, we recommend
that the Municipal Accountant refrain from processing payments for consultants
unless these are duly supported with complete documents to validate services
rendered and to record the payments in the proper expense account under Other
Professional Services account.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer
reconcile their records with the GSIS and communicate immediately to the said
agency any necessary change in the employees’ employment status to update the
records of the GSIS and tally with the LGU records on premium contributions.
We also recommended that the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO):
a) monitor approvals made on loan applications of employees and inform
the personnel in-charge with the payroll to ensure that deductions are
made when due, and;
b.) furnish monthly all payroll preparer with copies of the GSIS billing to
ensure that the correct amount of monthly premium contributions and
loan amortizations deducted in the payroll are based on the said GSIS
billing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru the Sanggunian Bayan
appropriate in the Annual Budget funds for priority programs and activities for
Solid Waste Management such as development of dumpsites, acquisition of
additional garbage trucks and establishment of MRF for waste disposal, and
require the MENRO to utilize the amount in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Local School Board Council incorporate in its
budget programs and activities intended for Early Childhood Care Development.

We recommended that the Mayor as the Chairman of the municipality’s
Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC) initiate the preparation of the
following documents for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual
Budget: a.) Council’s Annual Work and Financial Plan prioritized according to
needs for integration in the local development plan; b.) Local Development Plan
for Children (LDPC); c.) Local Investment Plan for Children (LCC).
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer ensure that the
programs and activities to be attributed as LCPC as contained in the Council’s
AWFP is equivalent to one percent (1%) of the IRA in the Annual Budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer exert effort to locate the paid but missing disbursement vouchers and record the same in the books of the LGU for immediate submission to COA. The Municipal Treasurer ensure that all paid DVs are in his custody to avoid occurrence of losses and audit disallowances.

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant record the reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements, especially those based on the bank statements in order to reflect an accurate balance of cash in its depository bank.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the other LGU officials concerned: (1) review and revise ordinances relative to the operations of these economic enterprises to intensify the LGUs revenue–generating capacities; (2) devise income-generating activities, and (3) limit expenditures to the most basic and necessary, in order to attain a more sound financial performance of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to restore the balances of the fully-depreciated Property, Plant and Equipment which were closed at yearend. Require the General Services Officer first to determine whether future economic benefits or service potential can still be expected from the utilization of these equipment and if found, none, recommend to the Accounting Office the derecognition of the same.

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant include in the Notes to the Financial Statements for PPE accounts  disclosures on the reconciliation of the carrying amount at the beginning and end of the period showing the following: additions, increases or decreases resulting from revaluations, impairment losses (if applicable) and other changes.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and other LGU personnel concerned to fast-track the completion of the projects and programs in accordance with the timeline provided for in the MOA.  Also, submit the required reports to the funding agencies concerned, specifically the Liquidation Reports, and return any excess funds to the Bureau of Treasury after completion of the project, if required in the MOA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to  fast- track the implementation of projects during the year and ensure that the development projects paid out of the 20% IRA is optimally utilized.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor and the Local Development Council appropriate the 20% Development Fund for projects which are capital in nature and those that will contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

We also recommend that the Local Development Council and other LGU officials involved in the formulation of the Annual Investment Plan to indicate therein the appropriate classification of each project and include the timeline for its implementation.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant determine the amount due to each barangay, both from the General Fund and Trust Fund and prepare the accompanying Disbursement Vouchers for approval of the Municipal Mayor.

We also recommend that the Municipal Treasurer issue checks in favor of the concerned barangays based on the DV prepared in order to remit all RPT shares due them immediately. Also, transfer immediately to the Trust Fund succeeding RPT shares of the barangay and remit to the barangay on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter without any deductions thereon for whatever purpose.

We recommend that the Local School Board include in the SEF budget only those authorized in DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular and consider as topmost priority the improvement of educational facilities in order to maximize the use of the Special Education Fund for the purpose for which it is intended.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and the MDRRMO to submit justification why the unexpended LDRRMF in CY 2011 to CY 2015 were not transferred to the Special Trust Fund each year and why the cash pertaining thereto was utilized for other purposes.

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant restore in the books of the LGU the Trust Liabilities- DRRM account amount pertaining to the unexpended LDRRMF in prior years and to avoid preparing accounting entries without sufficient basis.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal DRRMF Officer to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM fund  in accordance with its  intended purpose so as to fully achieve the government’s objective of appropriating the fund solely for enhancing  disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the municipality.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Council, through the MDDRMO, to prepare the LDRRMFIP annually in the prescribed format, with complete information and details on the specific programs/projects and activities to be implemented for the year including those to be funded from the unexpended balances of prior years’ LDRRMF.

We recommend that the MDRRM Officer submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF within the reglementary period and include in the report all information required in Annex B of COA Circular 2012-002 to enable COA and other offices concerned to conduct evaluation of the LDRRMF utilization on time.

We recommend  that the Municipal Mayor comply with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 on the appropriations, grant and utilization of confidential funds to avoid audit suspensions and disallowances.

We recommend that the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that the posting requirements on Request for Quotations for procurement with ABCs above P50,000.00 are adhered to by the BAC Secretariat.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive: (a) provide the necessary support in encouraging barangays to establish their own MRFs and to fully implement waste segregation at source; (b) strictly enforce and intensify the information campaign on the no-littering policy among the residents; and (c) consider embarking on effective projects and programs to support the LGUs ecological solid waste management program.
